description:
You are acting as a penetration-tester who has just copied the target’s installed-package
    database onto your workstation for quick, offline analysis.  
    The database dump is already on disk as:

        /home/user/pkg_scan/installed_packages.txt

    Contents of that text file follow the normal, column-based output you would get from
    a typical “dpkg -l” listing (five columns separated by one or more spaces):

        <status> <package> <version> <arch> <description>

    Example line (taken straight from the file you have):
        ii  openssl  1.1.1f-1ubuntu2  amd64  Secure Sockets Layer toolkit – cryptographic utility

    YOUR GOAL  
    1. Read /home/user/pkg_scan/installed_packages.txt.  
    2. Pull out only the version strings for the two packages listed below:
          • openssl  
          • nmap  
    3. Write those two version strings to the new file

          /home/user/pkg_scan/package_versions.log

       using *exactly* the following two-line format (ASCII, UTF-8, newline at the end):

          openssl version: <VERSION_STRING>
          nmap version: <VERSION_STRING>

       Replace “<VERSION_STRING>” with the values you extracted.  
       Do NOT add additional whitespace, lines, or comments.  
       The log file must end with a single trailing newline (the automated checker
       will look for it).

    4. Finally, display the contents of /home/user/pkg_scan/package_versions.log to the
       terminal so the user can immediately see the result.

    HINTS  
    • No super-user rights are needed; everything is in your home directory.  
    • Pure shell built-ins plus grep/awk/sed are enough; the entire job can be done in no
      more than three commands.  

    WHAT THE TESTER WILL VERIFY  
    • The file /home/user/pkg_scan/package_versions.log exists.  
    • Its contents match the exact two-line format shown above.  
    • Lines are in the specified order (openssl first, nmap second).  
    • A final newline is present.  
    • You echoed the file’s contents to the terminal.

    When you are finished, you should have created exactly one new file
    (/home/user/pkg_scan/package_versions.log) and shown its contents on screen.
truth:
The initial environment (before the agent starts):

    1. Directory /home/user/pkg_scan already exists and is writable by user.
    2. File /home/user/pkg_scan/installed_packages.txt exists with these five relevant lines
       among others (the rest of the file may contain additional packages; they can be ignored):

        ii  openssl  1.1.1f-1ubuntu2  amd64  Secure Sockets Layer toolkit – cryptographic utility
        ii  curl     7.68.0-1ubuntu2  amd64  command line tool for transferring data with URL syntax
        ii  nmap     7.80+dfsg1-2     amd64  The Network Mapper
        ii  lynx     2.9.0-1          amd64  Text-mode WWW Browser
        ii  nano     4.8-1ubuntu1     amd64  small, friendly text editor inspired by Pico

    Required end state for automatic checking:

    • File /home/user/pkg_scan/package_versions.log must exist, permissions 0644
      (default umask is fine).

    • Exact file contents (including trailing newline):

        openssl version: 1.1.1f-1ubuntu2
        nmap version: 7.80+dfsg1-2

    • No other files need to be changed or created.
name: task_000000_a2e4cf74
